Context: RESOURCE  1    2  High resolution, proteome-wide mapping of subcellular protein localization in plants  3    4  Monique van Schie1, Mark Roosjen1,*, Catherine Albrecht1,   5  Jarik van Marsdijk1 and Dolf Weijers1,*  6    7  1, Laboratory of Biochemistry, Wageningen University, Stippeneng 4, 6708WE Wageningen,  8  the Netherlands  9  * Corresponding authors: mark.roosjen@wur.nl, dolf.weijers@wur.nl  10  Summary   11    12  Protein function is intimately connected to subcellular localization, and experimental  13  determination of protein localization is a key element of understanding biological roles.  14  However, even in the best-studied model plants, such as Arabidopsis thaliana, a minority of  15  proteins has an experimentally defined subcellular localization. We present an experimental  16  strategy to globally map plant subcellular proteomes by mass spectrometry. Context: 16  strategy to globally map plant subcellular proteomes by mass spectrometry. We annotated  17  subcellular localization of 7815 proteins in Arabidopsis roots, 4672 in Arabidopsis seedlings,  18  and 2782 in the liverwort Marchantia polymorpha. By independent validation, we find that  19  these annotations are highly predictive and can be integrated with other proteomics datasets.  20  Cross-species comparisons reveal substantial global conservation of subcellular localization.  21  Furthermore, we demonstrate that the same approach can be used to identify dynamically  22  translocating proteins upon treatment or in a mutant. This work shows the power of global  23  spatial proteome mapping in plants and offers an extensive resource for protein subcellular  24  localization in plants.  25  . Context: Introduction  40    41  In eukaryotic cells, the subcellular localization of proteins is tightly connected to their function.  42  The different microenvironments within the various compartments of the cell not only offer  43  optimized conditions for specific protein functions, but also offer the potential to concentrate  44  or separate proteins1. Hence, translocation of proteins is often involved in biological processes2.  45  Studying subcellular localization and protein translocations at a cell-wide scale is therefore  46  crucial to our understanding of protein functions and their role in cellular processes3.   47  Over the past decades, numerous approaches have been developed to study subcellular  48  localization of proteins. Among these are microscopy-based methods that require the  49  fluorescent tagging of proteins, or the use of antibody staining, offering high precision but low  50

Context: 48  localization of proteins. Among these are microscopy-based methods that require the  49  fluorescent tagging of proteins, or the use of antibody staining, offering high precision but low  50  throughput4. Isolation of single organelles followed by mass spectrometry is also widely used,  51  which offers a higher throughput compared to microscopy, but often proteins from other  52  organelles are co-purified5. More recently, proximity ligation has been proven to be a powerful  53  tool to identify proteins in cellular niches6,7. These methods all offer specific benefits, but do  54  not provide a global overview of the cell.   55  To obtain this global overview of protein subcellular localization, multiple methods have been  56  established that rely on differential protein profiling. All these approaches are based on the  57  assumption that proteins will behave very similar if they are derived from the same subcellular  58

Context: 57  assumption that proteins will behave very similar if they are derived from the same subcellular  58  location8–11. In general, a gentle native lysis is followed by a separation of the cellular  59  compartments based on either physical or chemical properties. Separation is for example  60  achieved based on relative weight by the application of differential centrifugation8 or density  61  gradients9. Furthermore, separation based on differences in chemical properties10 or by the use  62  of labeled proteins pulled down with antibodies11 have been demonstrated. These efforts have  63  yielded many new biological insights, for example on protein trafficking related diseases and  64  cellular responses to viral infections12,13.   65  For plants, information on subcellular localization of proteins is more fragmented. There are  66  several examples of proteomes of individual organelles such as mitochondria14–18, vacuoles19– 67

Context: For plants, information on subcellular localization of proteins is more fragmented. There are  66  several examples of proteomes of individual organelles such as mitochondria14–18, vacuoles19– 67  21, chloroplasts 22,23, the endomembrane system24–28, cytosol29, and nucleus30,31, but there is only  68  a single example of a global profiling of subcellular localization that encompasses 527  69  Arabidopsis proteins32. Hence, there is little insight in global plant proteome organization,  70  likely mostly because of challenges posed by the very nature of plant tissues. Plants have rigid  71  . Furthermore, plant proteins are generally not as well annotated compared to those  73  in other model species, hence identification rates tend to be lower and as a consequence, there  74  are fewer well-validated subcellular marker proteins34.   75  We here describe a strategy building on existing protein profiling methods based on differential  76  centrifugation approaches, which we optimized for use on plant tissues. We optimized the  77  complete workflow including sample preparation, data acquisition and downstream data  78  processing and analysis. We present a resource deep subcellular proteome of the flowering plant  79  Arabidopsis thaliana, both at the organ level for roots and the organismal level for seedlings.  80  Furthermore, we compare the Arabidopsis subcellular proteome to that of the liverwort  81

Lastly, all the presented data, including the  86  translocation data sets, is interactively browsable in a set of Shiny apps through the following  87  links: Roots, Seedling, Marchantia, BFA treatment, gn-fwr.  88  . Context: Results  89    90  An optimized procedure for global mapping of subcellular protein localization in  91  Arabidopsis  92  No established methods are yet available for deep, global mapping of subcellular protein  93  localization in plants. We therefore compared two similar methods that are commonly used in  94  other eukaryotic species for their applicability to plants8,38. Both Localization of Organelle  95  Proteins by Isotope Tagging after Differential Centrifugation (LOPIT-DC) and Dynamic  96  Organellar Maps (DOMs) methods are based on the separation of organelles by differential  97  centrifugation, followed by protein identification and quantification in the individual fractions  98  by mass spectrometry8,38. The underlying principle is that instead of isolating organelles  99  individually, the cell contents are fractionated in a pattern where every organelle or  100  compartment displays a unique distribution pattern over the fractions. These patterns can then  101

We found DIA to  121  increase the identified number of protein groups by 60%, while also improving the  122  reproducibility of the data (Figure S2).   123  Having established a reliable lysis and fractionation method for Arabidopsis roots, we next  124  explored data analysis and visualization. Normalized MS quantifications for each protein across  125  all fractions of three replicate experiments were projected on a UMAP39 (Figure 1B). Next,  126  clusters of proteins with similar distribution patterns were statistically identified by the  127  algorithm HDBSCAN40 (Figure 1B). Afterwards, the identity of each cluster was determined  128  using well-established marker proteins, based on experimentally validated microscopy  129  localizations in the SUBA5 database41, supplemented by experimentally derived information  130  from the Uniprot database42 where necessary. Context: Figure 1: Spatial proteomes of Arabidopsis roots and seedlings   159  (A) Overview of the method for generating spatial proteomic maps. Plant tissues are lysed and  160  contents are separated by differential centrifugation into 10 fractions. The proteins in each  161  fraction are identified and quantified by mass spectrometry. Protein abundance is normalized  162  over fractions, resulting in unique patterns. All proteins in the dataset are visualized by UMAP  163  based on their fractionation patterns and marker proteins are used to annotate regions of the  164  map. (B) Overview of the procedure to identify the subcellular localization of clusters in the  165  UMAP to curate the marker protein list. First, clusters are identified in a data-driven manner  166  using the HDBSCAN algorithm. Next, proteins in every cluster are checked for experimental  167  evidence of subcellular localization. Lastly, clusters are curated based on the quality of the  168

We participated in all the three tracks for Indic languages only (Hindi, Bengali, Malayalam) under a non-constrained and proprietary multi- lingual and multimodal Large Language Model (LLM): Chitranuvad1. In this paper, we provide a description of our multimodal LLM where we leverage a multi- lingual LLM backbone Krutrim (Kallappa et al., 2024), coupled with a visual image encoder. Currently, PALO (Maaz et al., 2024) is a multimodal LLM that supports only Hindi and Bengali. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no other open-source multi- modal LLMs trained specifically for low-resource Indic languages. In contrast, we developed a multi- lingual multimodal translation system that supports 10 Indic languages based on our general purpose multimodal model Chitrarth (Khan et al., 2025). 3 Model and Training Recipes Figure 2 provides an overview of our architec- ture and the multi-stage training pipeline. Our Krutrim LLM is trained across 10 languages and natively supports all the 3 Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, Malay- alam) used as part of the shared task. For the multimodal training, we first encode im- ages through a vision encoder. Next, the modal- ity projection (adapter/connector) layer projects the vision embeddings into the LLM embedding space, creating a sequence of visual tokens. The multi-lingual LLM then generates the response con- ditioned on these visual embedding tokens. The Krutrim LLM model supports a context length of 4096 tokens, out of which 576 tokens are used for the image representation, obtained after the modal- ity projector layer. Stage 1: In our initial experiments, we use the LLaVA-Pretrain-LCS-558K data for pre-training our model in Stage 1. However, recent works (Tong et al., 2024) showed that more adapter data is bene- ficial for the model, such as the 1.2M ShareGPT4V- PT (Chen et al., 2023) image-captioning dataset, which we use in Stage 1 training. We also trans- lated this data in the 10 Indic languages that our LLM natively supports, using an in-house text Ma- chine Translation system. We sample translations across different languages (including English) in an equal ratio and ensure that PT data limits to 1.2M data points in our final data mix. However, we found a de- creased performance against including the labels of all the detected objects.

Context: LoRA vs Full finetuning. We investigated the efficacy of full fine-tuning versus Low-Rank Adap- tation (LoRA) using Visual Genome data. Our ex- periments (see Table 7) reveal that full fine-tuning consistently outperforms LoRA, i.e. LoRA learns less (Biderman et al., 2024). Bi-lingual vs Multi-lingual For Stage 3 training, we experiment with training specialized models for each language (Hindi and Bengali) compared to multi-lingual setting with a mix of data from all the three Indic languages. We didn’t find any im- provement over multi-lingual model but instead observe catastrophic forgetting (Zhai et al., 2023c; Tong et al., 2024), where the translation abilities of the model in the other languages deteriorate com- pletely. Context: We present Chitranuvad, a multimodal LLM that is adapted for image grounded Machine translation. Our model encodes images using a pre-trained im- age encoder (Alexey, 2020) and translates the En- glish sentences autoregressively into different Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, Malayalam) using a pre- trained LLM. Empirically, our model outperforms previous baselines for different tasks. However, we also observed that vision modality had little impact on the translation, echoing the observations from (Grönroos et al., 2018; Lala et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). Broader Impact: We believe our work paves way for building next generation assistants which can do multimodal machine translation. We be- lieve these systems can empower different sectors like education, healthcare, banking and financial services, etc. to name a few. Limitations and Future Work: While this work is focused to three Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, Malayalam), we consider our approach as

Limitations and Future Work: While this work is focused to three Indic languages (Hindi, Bengali, Malayalam), we consider our approach as a first step towards building general purpose multi- lingual system which can handle various Indic lan- guages. While in our current setup, we freeze the vision encoder during training, recent works have shown that unfreezing the vision encoder with Per- ceiver Resampler (Jaegle et al., 2021), helps learn better representations (Laurençon et al., 2024; Tong et al., 2024), which we plan to explore in the future.
